data.title: AST SpaceMobile's Plan to Beat SpaceX's Starlink. What's Propelling the Stock. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

AST SpaceMobile was rising sharply on Wednesday. The company is bouncing back from a recent dip as it got a new investor and prepares to compete with Elon Musk's SpaceX in the field of satellite-to-cell communications.

AST SpaceMobile was rising 13% to $105.11 in early trading. That came after it announced Canadian communications firm Telus Corp. as a new partner and equity shareholder in the company late on Tuesday.

The stock has more than tripled in the 12 months but is notably volatile, having plunged from highs of more than $120 earlier this year to lows of less than $80 following a recent convertible debt issue.

AST SpaceMobile is racing to get enough satellites into space in order to deliver 5G-quality voice, data, and video coverage to cellphones anywhere in the world. In addition to Telus, it has agreements with dozens of major mobile network operators including AT&T and Verizon.

However, it is facing off against a major rival in the form of Musk's SpaceX. This week, SpaceX confirmed the rebranding of its own direct-to-device offering as "Starlink Mobile" in a presentation at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) telecoms conference in Barcelona. Accompanying the announcement, SpaceX said its next-generation satellites will deliver 5G speeds from space, with 100 times the data density of its current hardware.

Notably, Starlink Mobile is set to offer download speeds up to 150 megabits per second in ideal conditions, according to a presentation made at MWC by Michael Nicolls, SpaceX's senior vice president of Starlink engineering, on Monday.

That throws the gauntlet down to AST SpaceMobile, which has long suggested that its own satellites — which are much larger but fewer in number than the thousands of Starlink craft — will offer superior performance. AST SpaceMobile has said its own latest satellite is capable of exceeding peak data speeds of 120 megabits per second.

AST SpaceMobile President Scott Wisniewski told Barron's that he wasn't worried about Starlink Mobile's claims.

"In terms of stated speeds we'll let the live demonstrations speak for themselves," Wisniewski said, in an interview at MWC. "At the end of the day it's driven by how much spectrum you have...and the size of your satellite, and we're flying the largest ever communications array put into low Earth orbit."

Another key determining factor is likely to be whether AST SpaceMobile can get its satellites into orbit quickly enough to establish a commercial foothold before its giant rival catches up. With six satellites currently in orbit, it needs between 45 and 60 operational to offer a commercial service in northern latitudes, a goal it is aiming to achieve this year across 12 contracted rocket launches.

If all goes according to plan, that would mean AST SpaceMobile is operating in key markets ahead of its rival. SpaceX's Nicholls said that his company plans to launch its direct-to-cell capable satellites in mid-2027 and can put up a constellation capable of "global and contiguous coverage" within six months.

AST SpaceMobile's army of fervent retail trader fans will be hoping that by then, their own company's satellites are already tracing their own celestial path.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Stuart Rush Launches as an Independent Industrial Manufacturing Company Following Divestiture from Columbus McKinnon
data.description:
DAMASCUS, VA / ACCESS Newswire / March 4, 2026 / Stuart Rush today announced its official launch as a standalone industrial manufacturing company following its divestiture from Columbus McKinnon.

While the company name is new, the products, experienced teams, and long-standing distributor relationships remain in place. Stuart Rush will continue serving distributors and end users across industrial manufacturing and material handling applications.

"Our distributor partners and customers know this business, and they know our team," said Spencer Darr, President/CCO of Stuart Rush. "As we move forward as an independent company, our commitment to product quality, clear communication, and long-term relationships remains unchanged."

Stuart Rush manufactures industrial lifting and material handling products designed for reliable performance in demanding applications.

For more information, visit www.stuart-rush.com

Media Contacts:

Anthony Alessi

Director of Marketing & Analytics

Marketing@stuart-rush.com

Vicki Nazzaro

Customer Experience & Strategic Programs Manager

Marketing@stuart-rush.com

SOURCE: Stuart Rush

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Australian shares expected to open higher, NZ up in early trade
data.description:
Australian shares were likely to open higher on Thursday after losing more than 3% over the last two sessions, as investors assessed the widening Middle East conflict alongside strong domestic growth data from a day ago that could fan inflation.

The local share price index futures (YAPcm1) rose 1.1%, a 51.8-point premium to the underlying S&P/ASX 200 index close. The benchmark slipped 1.9% on Wednesday.

New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index rose 0.1% in early trade.

data.title: Baltic index edges lower on weak capesize rates
data.description:
The Baltic Exchange's dry bulk freight index, which monitors rates for ships carrying dry bulk commodities, edged lower on Wednesday, pressured by a weaker capesize segment.

* The main Baltic index , which tracks rates for capesize, panamax and supramax vessels, was down 9 points, or 0.4%, at 2,233.

* The capesize index (.BACI) lost 43 points, or 1.3%, to 3,202.

* Average daily earnings for capesize vessels (.BATCA), which typically transport 150,000-ton cargoes including iron ore and coal, decreased $388 to $25,541.

* Iron ore prices moved in a tight range on Wednesday, as investors and traders exercised caution ahead of the upcoming annual parliamentary meeting in China, the world's second-largest economy, from March 5.

* Meanwhile, the panamax index (.BPNI) rose 12 points, or 0.6%, to 2,014.

* Average daily earnings for panamax vessels (.BPWT), which usually carry 60,000 to 70,000 tons of coal or grain, rose $112 to $18,127.

* Among smaller vessels, the supramax index (.BSIS) advanced 9 points, or 0.7%, to 1,392.

data.title: Aquestive Therapeutics 10-K: Revenue $44.5M, Losses Pressure Operations
data.description:
Aquestive Therapeutics reported total revenues of $44.5 million for the year, a 23% decline year‑over‑year driven by lower license & royalty and proprietary product revenue, and referenced a net loss that contributed to increased cash used in operations. The filing discloses an operating loss of approximately $67.8 million and notes gross profit expressed in the filing. Management highlights ongoing work to advance its epinephrine prodrug toward potential FDA approval and prepare for a commercial launch.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenues: $44.5 million, decreased 23% year‑over‑year.
  • Gross profit: $25.99 million (reported in the filing).
  • Operating income (loss): $(67.79) million (approximate as disclosed in the filing).
  • Net income: Net loss is referenced in the filing and noted as a driver of increased cash used in operations (specific net income figure not provided in the excerpt).
  • Net income per share: Not provided in the provided excerpt.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue mix shift: Manufacture & supply revenue remained relatively stable (approximately flat), while declines in license & royalty and co‑development fees drove the overall revenue decrease.
  • Proprietary product channel changes: U.S. market access for Libervant ended in April 2025, reducing proprietary product revenue and prompting increased returns provisioning.
  • Pipeline advancement: The company is advancing its epinephrine prodrug (Anaphylm) toward potential FDA approval and is undertaking regulatory work and commercial launch preparations.
  • Manufacturing capacity and compliance: Owns manufacturing operations in Portage, Indiana; facilities have been inspected by FDA, TGA, and DEA and are positioned to meet current commercial volumes.
  • R&D focus: Research and development spending declined about 15% as clinical trial costs for Anaphylm decreased, while product research and personnel costs showed modest increases.

Original SEC Filing: Aquestive Therapeutics, Inc. [ AQST ] - 10-K - Mar. 04, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Amazon cuts more jobs; this time in robotics unit
data.description:
By Greg Bensinger

Amazon on Tuesday confirmed it laid off staff across its robotics unit, with at least 100 white-collar jobs affected, two people familiar with the matter told Reuters.

This comes after a January cut of about 16,000 jobs with the company at the time hinting layoffs would continue.

The division axed on Tuesday is responsible for designing robots and other conveyances for automation, primarily in warehouses.

"We regularly review our organizations to make sure teams are best set up to innovate and deliver for our customers," Amazon said in the statement, without specifying the number of jobs cut. Business Insider earlier reported the robotics cuts.

Starting with a round of some 14,000 white-collar employees in October, Amazon has trimmed 30,000 corporate employees, tying them to efficiency gains from artificial intelligence, as well as revising company culture. The layoffs represented nearly 10% of white-collar workers, though the bulk of Amazon's 1.5 million workers are hourly staff, particularly in warehouses known as fulfillment centers.

The latest job cuts come after Amazon in January halted development of a robotic arm known as Blue Jay that it demonstrated at an event in October. Blue Jay featured multiple robotic arms that could grab several items at once and was designed to help workers in smaller spaces.

In addition to the broader cuts in October and January, Amazon over the past year has pared a smaller number of jobs in its devices and services, books, podcasts and public relations units, among others.
